title: Evidence of Quantum Machine Learning Advantage with Tens of Noisy Qubits
authors: Onur Danaci, Yash J. Patel, Riccardo Molteni, Evert van Nieuwenburg, Vedran Dunjko, Jan A. Krzywda
abstract: Learning problems involving quantum data are natural candidates for demonstrating an advantage in quantum machine learning. Recent results indicate that, for certain tasks and under noiseless conditions, coherent processing of quantum data outperforms fixed-measurement schemes followed by classical processing. It remained uncertain whether this performance gap persists at a finite scale, and in the presence of noise that is unavoidable with current quantum devices. In this work, we present simulations and analysis of the performance of existing hardware on a learning problem known to exhibit asymptotic advantage, now subjected to noisy quantum data. Comparing coherent quantum processing directly against fixed-measurement schemes, our results demonstrate a clear performance separation at a scale of just 30 to 40 noisy qubits. Already at this scale, the fundamental bottleneck is no longer classical computation but data acquisition; matching the noisy coherent protocol with measure-first strategies would still require months or even years of measurements. By systematically evaluating hardware constraints such as state preparation, gate errors, readout errors, connectivity, and coherence times, we provide evidence that a demonstration of such a strong learning advantage is accessible on near-term devices.
summary: This paper shows that quantum machine learning can outperform classical measurement-based strategies even with 30 to 40 noisy qubits. It demonstrates that the primary bottleneck for classical methods is the massive amount of data acquisition required, making quantum advantage accessible on near-term hardware.
main_problem: Determining whether the theoretical quantum advantage in Quantum Machine Learning (QML) persists at finite scales and in the presence of realistic hardware noise.
main_result: Demonstrated a clear performance separation between Fully Quantum and Measure-First protocols at a scale of 30 to 40 noisy qubits, where classical data acquisition would require months or years to match quantum performance.
method: A scalable simulation pipeline for Fully Quantum protocols under hardware constraints, combined with a robust finite-size scaling extrapolation framework to predict large-scale performance.
model_or_system: A learning task involving n-qubit binary phase-states subject to various noise channels including dephasing, depolarizing, and thermal relaxation, modeled across different hardware architectures (all-to-all and square-lattice connectivity).
key_observables: Sample complexity (number of state copies required), accuracy, and the visibility of the interference pattern.
important_parameters: Qubit number (n_q), Hamming weight of the target state (|alpha|), preparation noise rate (epsilon_p), and hardware-specific error rates.
main_assumptions: The study uses a specific learning problem known to exhibit asymptotic advantage and assumes a phenomenological 1/n_q scaling for the extrapolation of classical resource requirements.
figures_summary: Schematics of FQ vs MF frameworks, scaling plots of state copies vs qubit number, accuracy curves under different noise channels, and validation plots for the extrapolation model.
paper_structure: Introduction of the QML advantage problem, description of the FQ and MF protocols, detailed noise and hardware modeling, simulation methodology, results for different noise channels, and a rigorous statistical validation of the extrapolation technique.
why_interesting: The paper provides a rigorous framework for predicting the onset of quantum advantage in noisy, near-term devices, which is critical for designing experiments in open quantum systems and evaluating the utility of NISQ-era hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Framework for evaluating quantum advantage in learning from noisy quantum data. Left: Schematic of the two competing paradigms for the learning task described in the main text. A noisy quantum state ˜ρ(f) is prepared subject to a local error channel. The fully quantum protocol (Sec. II) coherently processes the state through a potentially noisy entangling measurement circuit to extract the parity bit b. In contrast, measure-first protocols (Sec. III) immediately measure nc copies of the state to build a classical representation for subsequent statistical inference. Right: Representative scaling of the number of state copies nc required by classical measure-first methods to match the...

Extrapolating from simulations up to |α| = nq ≤22 (Appendix C), we observe a clear error transition in the nearest-neighbor devices (B and C): they shift from being gate-fidelity dominated at low |α| to idling-error domi- nated at |α| > 10. FIG. 3. Accuracy of the fully quantum protocol un- der realistic hardware constraints. The expected ac- curacy AQ is shown as a function of qubit number nq for device models A, B, and C (distinguished by color) and for three state-preparation noise channels (dephasing, depolariz- ing, and relaxation, distinguished by line style). The columns compare high (ϵp = 0.05, left) and low (ϵp = 0.01, right) preparation-noise rates, while the top and bottom rows corre- spond to concept sizes |α| = nq and |α| = nq/2, respectively. These results are obtained by extrapolating simulations of Vm at nq ≤22 (darker lines) to larger system sizes using the factorized error model VQ = VpVmVr. See Appendix C...

FIG. 4. Sample complexity of measure-first protocols. The expected accuracy is plotted against the scaled copy- complexity exponent k, defined by nc = 2knq, at preparation- noise rate ϵp = 0.1. The panels show (a) the Hypergraph method, (b) the Eigenshadow method, and the ML method for (c) |α| = nq and (d) |α| = nq/2. Noise channels are dis- tinguished by color (blue: dephasing, red: relaxation, green: depolarizing), and increasing system size (nq = 5 to 15) is indicated by increasing color intensity and line thickness.
